_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to define sin(x), cos(x) and tan(x) using the unit circle and graph these trigonometric functions.

_source.w: We are learning to define sin(x), cos(x) and tan(x) using the unit circle and graph these trigonometric functions.
y: The unit circle extends trigonometric ratios beyond acute angles to all angles, providing a complete definition for sin, cos and tan.
Graphing trigonometric functions reveals their periodic nature — essential for modelling waves, oscillations and cyclical phenomena.
These concepts are foundational for VCE Mathematics and physics, where trigonometric functions model everything from sound to electricity.
eg: Using the unit circle to find exact values: sin(30°) = ½, cos(60°) = ½, tan(45°) = 1.
Graphing y = sin(x) and identifying its amplitude (1), period (360°) and key features.
Comparing the graphs of y = sin(x) and y = cos(x) and describing their phase relationship.
Extending to angles beyond 90°: sin(150°) = sin(30°) = ½.
Identifying the signs of sin, cos and tan in each quadrant using the ASTC rule.
Exploring how changing amplitude and period affects the graph: y = 2sin(3x).
